coredata.dc:title: Red emissive N, Cl-doped carbon dots for detection of tea polyphenols and lysosomal imaging at the cellular level
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Lei
Zhu, Tongtong
Zan, Minghui
Liu, Yulu
Xing, Xiaoman
Qian, Qing
Mei, Qian
Dong, Wen-Fei
Li, Li
coredata.dc:description: Tea polyphenols (TP), which is accounting for 15–30% of the dry weight of green tea and are widely used in the food industry as antioxidants, preservatives, color preservatives, and flavoring agents. To determine the concentration of TP, a fluorescent method based on nitrogen and chlorine co-doped carbon dots (R-CDs) was investigated. The R-CDs we prepared showed red emission with an absolute quantum yield (QY) of approximately 16.67 ± 0.85%. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be approximately 68 ± 0.94 ng/mL, with an R2 of 0.999 over the TP range of 1–20 μg/mL. Compared to the ferrous tartrate standard curve method, the concentration of TP in green tea, which was purchased from the market, detected using our method showed an error within 5%, indicating good potential applications in the food industry. Cell imaging experiments suggested that the R-CDs exhibit excellent lysosomal targeting as the Pearson’s correlation coefficient (PCC) in colocalization analysis was found to be 0.91. Further experiments showed that owing to their excitation-independent emission and high QY, the R-CDs could be applied to not only for detecting TP content but also for targeting lysosomes as substitute for commercial dyes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Tea polyphenols
Limit of detection
Lysosomal targeting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of the R-CDs
Detection of TP by the R-CDs
Cytotoxicity and lysosomal targeting study of the R-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (Grand No. 2018YFC2001100 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grand No. 21803075 , 91959112 , 81902166 , 62027825 , and 82172077 ), the Youth Innovation Promotion Association CAS ( 2022326 ), the Management Committee of Chongqing Liangjiang New Area ( E1050J73 ) and the High-level Innovation and Entrepreneurship Talent Introduction Plan of Jiangsu Province ( JSSCBS20211442 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HR-TEM (inset) images of the R-CDs. (b) Size distribution of the R-CDs, with an average diameter of 2.90 nm. (c) The FTIR spectra of the R-CDs and 4-chlorobenzene-1,2-diamine. (d) XPS spectrum of the R-CDs. (e-h) C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s, and Cl 2p spectra of the R-CDs.

(a) The UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of the R-CDs. (b) Fluorescence spectra of the R-CDs. (c) CIE color coordinates of the R-CDs, the color coordinates are (0.639, 0.360). (d) Total luminescence spectrum (200–900 nm) of the R-CDs.

(a) Fluorescent spectra of the R-CDs with the addition of different concentrations of TP at an excitation wavelength of 540 nm. (b) The linear relationship between (F
0 /F - 1) and the concentration of TP over the range of 1– 20 μg/mL.

(a) The UV–vis absorption of the R-CDs, TP with the addition of ferrous tartrate and R-CDs with the addition of TP and ferrous tartrate. (b) Lifetimes of the R-CDs and R-CDs with the addition of TP. (c) Hydrodynamic size of the R-CDs with the addition of TP (689.90 nm). (d) Zeta potentials of the R-CDs, TP with the addition of ferrous tartrate, and the R-CDs with the addition of TP and ferrous tartrate.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the R-CDs with the addition of 130 μM of various interfering substances (Al3+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Br-, Glu, GSH, L-cysteine, L-tyrosine, and TP). (b) The corresponding fluorescence intensities of the R-CDs with the addition of the interfering substances. (c) The fluorescence intensities of the R-CDs with the addition of the interfering substances or the interfering substances and TP.

Laser scanning confocal microscopy images of the HeLa cells co-incubated with the R-CDs, Lyso-Tracker Green and DAPI dihydrochloride. (a-d) HeLa cells incubated with 50 μg/mL R-CDs. (e-h) HeLa cells incubated with 50 μg/mL R-CDs and 60 μg/mL TP. (i-l) HeLa cells incubated with 50 μg/mL R-CDs and 120 μg/mL TP. (Scale bar: 10 µm. From left to right: blue channel (λ = 405 nm), green channel (λ = 488 nm), red channel (λ = 543 nm), and Merged images.).
Schematic diagram of the preparation process of the R-CDs, which can be used to detect TP and for lysosomal targeting.
Analytical results for the detection of TP in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Red emissive N, Cl-doped carbon dots for detection of tea polyphenols and lysosomal imaging at the cellular level

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biomedical Engineering (Suzhou), Division of Life Sciences and Medicine, University of Science and Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tea polyphenols (TP), which is accounting for 15–30% of the dry weight of green tea and are widely used in the food industry as antioxidants, preservatives, color preservatives, and flavoring agents. To determine the concentration of TP, a fluorescent method based on nitrogen and chlorine co-doped carbon dots (R-CDs) was investigated. The R-CDs we prepared showed red emission with an absolute quantum yield (QY) of approximately 16.67 ± 0.85%. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be approximately 68 ± 0.94 ng/mL, with an R2 of 0.999 over the TP range of 1–20 μg/mL. Compared to the ferrous tartrate standard curve method, the concentration of TP in green tea, which was purchased from the market, detected using our method showed an error within 5%, indicating good potential applications in the food industry. Cell imaging experiments suggested that the R-CDs exhibit excellent lysosomal targeting as the Pearson’s correlation coefficient (PCC) in colocalization analysis was found to be 0.91. Further experiments showed that owing to their excitation-independent emission and high QY, the R-CDs could be applied to not only for detecting TP content but also for targeting lysosomes as substitute for commercial dyes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: China is well-known as the hometown of tea, which was founded by Shennong in mythology and can be traced back to more than 5000 years ago. In a way, tea from China has changed the world. For example, since the 17th century, the mysterious drinks from the east have affected the living habits of the British upper class and even ordinary people. The initiator of this social phenomenon was Princess Catherine. Even today, people in England still maintain the habit of drinking tea, and the per capita tea consumption in the UK has always remained among the top three in the world. Meanwhile, the other two countries in the top three, Turkey and Ireland, are influenced by the Silk Road or the British aristocracy. Tea polyphenols, a general term for polyphenols in tea [1], consist of catechins, flavonoids, anthocyanins, and phenolic acids [2]. TP are known to exhibit various physiological activities, such as anti-radiation [3], anti-aging [4], hypolipidemic [5], antioxidant [6], hypoglycemic [7], and antibacterial activities [8]. Among the compounds found in TP, catechins, including epicatechin (EC), epigallocatechin (EGC), epicatechin gallate (ECG) and epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), are the major compounds that present in TP and have the highest concentration [9,10]. EGCG, a major polyphenol in green tea, has been reported to have the potential to prevent carcinogenesis [11]. Owing to their beneficial health effects on the human body and excellent antioxidant properties, TP have become common additives in the food industry, making it important to determine their concentration in the foods that we consumed.

The TP content in tea depends on many factors, such as the tea varieties, the locations of tea trees, the tea harvesting season, and even the altitude at which tea trees grow [12]. Several methods can be used to determine the concentration of TP, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [13], near-infrared spectroscopy [14], ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometry [15], flow injection analysis (FIA) [16], electrochemistry method [17], and fluorescence spectrophotometry [18]. However, compared to fluorescence spectrophotometry, other methods may require the use of expensive equipment, time-consuming methodology, professional operators, and gave low-precision results. Thus, the detection of TP using fluorescence spectrophotometry appears to be an appropriate method.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial first reported in 2004, exhibit bright fluorescence [19]. Further research has indicated that CDs have excellent photostability [20], high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) [21], good biocompatibility [22], low toxicity, and economical [23,24]. Therefore, many sensors have been designed using CDs to detect metal ions [25], negative ions [26], and bioactive molecules [27,28] as well as to measure pH [29]. For example, Li et al. designed bright blue emissive CDs using a two-step hydrothermal method, and the synthesized CDs could respond to the different concentrations of calcium ions [30]. Yang et al. prepared nitrogen-doped CDs using the solvothermal method of o-phenylenediamine, and further experiments suggested that the CDs could combine with glutathione (GSH) to form a non-fluorescent complex that eventually quenched the fluorescence of CDs [31]. Moreover, Liu et al. synthesized pH-responsive CDs with red emission using the hydrothermal method of p-phenylenediamine, o-phenylenediamine, and dopamine. The red-emissive CDs (R-CDs) can aggregate and form nanoparticles of various sizes with increasing pH values (1.0–9.0) [32]. The fluorescence quenching, caused by the aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) effect, endowed the R-CDs with a good linear relationship with pH over the range of 3.5–6.5. Which is confirmed by cell and zebrafish imaging. Thus, CDs are potential sensors for many applications in various fields.

In this study, nitrogen and chlorine co-doped CDs (R-CDs) with bright red fluorescence were prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method using 4-chlorobenzene-1,2-diamine, diluted hydrochloric acid (~1.4 M), and deionized water ( Scheme 1). The maximum emission of the prepared CDs was 601 nm with a shoulder peak at 650 nm when the excitation wavelength was 540 nm. To our surprise, the fluorescence showed excitation-independent emission with an absolute QY as high as 16.67 ± 0.85%. This is the first reported nitrogen and chlorine co-doped CDs with a high PLQY for red fluorescence. To detect the TP content, the addition of Fe2+ could result in the formation of form new complexes, with strong absorption at 540 nm, via the coordination of Fe2+ to TP. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be approximately 68 ± 0.94 ng/mL. Moreover, the test results of the real sample indicated that the detection by fluorescence had an error within 5% compared with the standard curve method of ferrous tartrate. These results demonstrated that the prepared R-CDs we prepared to have potential applications for detecting TP in real samples. Further research demonstrated that the fluorescence quenching caused by TP originated from the inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching process. Moreover, HeLa cells were selected for cell imaging, and the high PCC in colocalization analysis (0.91) indicated that the R-CDs have bright red fluorescence with high lysosomal targeting. With the exogenous addition of TP, the fluorescence in HeLa cells was effectively quenched. These results suggested that the R-CDs were successfully applied to detect TP in real samples and have potential applications in the specific imaging of lysosomes.

4-Chlorobenzene-1,2-diamine, TP, KNaC4H12O10·4H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, Na2HPO4·12H2O, KH2PO4, chlorpromazine hydrochloride (CPZ), genistein, methyl-β-cyclodextrin (MβCD), amiloride, AlCl3, MnCl2, CuSO4, FeSO4, KCl, MgSO4, (CH3COO)2Zn, NaBr, glucose (Glu), glutathione (GSH), L-cysteine, L-tyrosine, absolute alcohol, dichloromethane (DCM), and methanol were purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai). Fetal bovine serum (FBS), Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 medium, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and trypsin were procured from Thermo Fisher. 2-(4-Amidinophenyl)− 6-indolecarbamidine (DAPI) dihydrochloride, 2-(4-iodophenyl)-3-(4-nitrophenyl)-5-(2,4-disulfophenyl)-2 H-tetrazolium sodium salt (WST-1), penicillin-streptomycin solution, 4% paraformaldehyde and Lyso-Tracker Green were obtained from Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). The green tea and tea drinks samples were purchased from a market.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the R-CDs were recorded using a transmission electron microscope (JEOL-2100 F). The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a Nicolet IS-10 FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (AXIS ULTRA DLD). The UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a UV–vis absorption spectrometer (Agilent Cary 300 Scan). The zeta potential and hydrodynamic size of the samples were determined using a Zetasizer Nano ZS-90 analyzer (Malvern Instruments). The absolute QY and lifetime were determined using an FLS1000 instrument (Edinburgh Instruments). The fluorescence spectra were obtained using an F97 Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer. Cell imaging was performed using a Leica TCS-SP8 confocal microscope.

The R-CDs were prepared using a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, 2 mM of 4-chlorobenzene-1,2-diamine was dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water. After sonication for 10 min, the mixture became clear. Then, 20 mL of diluted hydrochloric acid (~1.4 M) was added, and the mixture was transferred to a 100-mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Subsequently, the autoclave was heated at 180 ℃ and maintained for 10 h. When the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, a green solution containing R-CDs was obtained. After elution using a mixture of DCM and methanol (10:1), a bright red liquid was obtained and dried using a rotary evaporator. The solid R-CDs obtained after drying were stored in the dark at 4 ℃ for future characterization and use.

The buffer (pH = 7.5) was prepared by mixing with 17 mL of 0.067 M Na2HPO4 and 3 mL of 0.067 M KH2PO4. The ferrous tartrate solution was prepared by dissolving 0.1 g ferrous sulfate heptahydrate and 0.5 g sodium phosphate dibasic dodecahydrate in 100 mL of deionized water. To prepare the TP solution, 20 mg TP was dissolved in 10 mL of absolute alcohol. The TP sample was prepared by mixing 1 mL of TP solution, 1 mL of ferrous tartrate solution, and 8 mL of buffer. Different concentrations of TP (1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 μg/mL) were added to the 50 μg/mL R-CDs solution. The fluorescence spectra of the samples were then recorded.

For real samples analysis, the green tea purchased from the market was first mashed, and then the TP were extracted by dissolving 0.5 g of green tea powders in 20 mL of 70% ethanol solution. Next, the mixture was ultrasonicated for 30 min at 50 ℃ in a sealed state and filtered to remove the solid impurities. Subsequently, the tea sample obtained was diluted to 40 mL by adding 70% ethanol solution. The treatment of tea drinks were similar to that of green tea, and the detection of the samples was similar to that of TP.

To evaluate the cytotoxicity of the R-CDs, WST-1 assays were been performed. Briefly, the HeLa cells were plated at 104 cells per well in 96-well plates. The culture medium consisted of 10% FBS, 100 U/mL penicillin-streptomycin solution, and 90% RPMI 1640 medium. After 24 h of incubation, the medium was removed and a fresh complete medium containing different concentrations of the R-CDs (dissolved in DMSO, 5 mg/mL) was added and incubated for another 24 h. Finally, the medium was removed, the cells were washed three times with PBS, and cell viability was assessed using WST-1 assays. The absorption of all plates was recorded using a microplate reader at a wavelength of 450 nm.

To evaluate the effect of the R-CDs on cell imaging, HeLa cells were seeded into a 35-mm glass-bottom culture dish and cultured at 37 ℃ in a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2. After 24 h of incubation, the initial medium was removed and then 100 μg/mL of the R-CDs with 2 mL of fresh medium were incubated for another 6 h. Subsequently, the medium was removed, 1 mL of fresh medium was added into a dish containing Lyso-Tracker Green and different concentrations of TP, and cultured at 37 ℃ for 1 h. Next, the cells were washed three times with PBS and refilled with 4% paraformaldehyde. DAPI dihydrochloride was used for nuclear targeting. Furthermore, the effect of incubation time and five kinds of inhibitors (4 ℃, CPZ, genistein, MβCD, and amiloride) were used for studying the mechanism of lysosomal targeting by the R-CDs. The cell imaging experiments and colocalization analysis were performed using a Leica TCS-SP8 confocal microscope and Image J, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The R-CDs were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method using 4-chlorobenzene-1,2-diamine, diluted HCl (~1.4 M), and deionized water. As we know, the purification method is very important for separating crude products and column chromatography is widely used to obtain high-purity CDs [33,34]. After purification by column chromatography, CDs typically achieved a narrow emission of fluorescence [35,36]. The morphology of the prepared R-CDs after purification was examined by TEM. The TEM images ( Fig. 1a, b) show that the R-CDs have a good dispersion with an average diameter of 2.90 nm by calculating 100 particles. The inset in Fig. 1a shows the HR-TEM image, which is similar to that reported in the literature [37,38], indicating that the R-CDs have an obvious crystal lattice with a d-spacing of 0.243 nm, which is attributed to the (1120) plane of the graphitic facet. The HR-TEM image as well as the fast Fourier transform (FFT) and inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT) results (Fig. S1a-d) confirmed the successful fabrication of R-CDs. The FTIR spectra of the R-CDs and the precursor 4-chlorobenzene-1,2-diamine are shown in Fig. 1c. The peaks at 3373, 3053, 2965, and 2927 cm−1 that are assigned to the stretching vibrations of N–H and C–H in the R-CDs. The stretching vibrations of CO, CN, CC, C–O, and C–Cl were observed at 1609, 1563, 1520, 1273, and 740 cm−1, respectively. Furthermore, the FTIR spectrum obtained for 4-chlorobenzene-1,2-diamine is consistent with that reported in previous studies [39,40]. The XPS spectrum of the R-CDs, shown in Fig. 1d, reveals the presence of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and chlorine in the sample. High-resolution XPS spectra were obtained to determine the elemental states. Based on the C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1e), the peaks at 284.8, 285.0, 286.0, and 287.0 eV are attributed to the C–C/CC, C–N/C–O, C–O–C, and C–Cl/CO structures, respectively. The O 1 s XPS spectrum of the R-CDs (Fig. 1f) shows two states of oxygen: CO (531.6 eV) and C–O (532.5 eV). The N 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1g) was deconvoluted into three peaks at 398.8 eV (pyridinic N), 400.5 eV (graphitic N), and 400.9 eV (C–NH2). As shown in Fig. 1h, there are four peaks at 196.3, 197.9, 199.5, and 201.1 eV, which correspond to C–Cl, Cl 2p3/2, Cl 2p, and Cl 2p1/2. Additionally, the EDX spectrum in Fig. S2 indicates the presence of C, N, O, and Cl in the R-CDs after purification. These results demonstrated the successful preparation of R-CDs.

The optical properties of the prepared R-CDs were determined by UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometry. As shown in Fig. 2a, the peak at 204 nm is attributed to the n–σ * transition of C–NH. The strong absorption band at 294 nm was ascribed to the π–π * transition of CC. Several absorption bands at approximately 400–600 nm were assigned to the n–π * transition of CN and CO [41]. Moreover, the excitation spectrum and emission spectra indicated that the R-CDs possessed strong emission at 601 nm with a shoulder peak at 650 nm and a maximum excitation wavelength of 540 nm. The inset shows the R-CDs under daylight and blue light (λ = 488 nm), demonstrating that the R-CDs emitted bright red fluorescence. The fluorescence spectra and the Commission Internationale de l′Eclairage (CIE) coordinates (0.639, 0.360) shown in Fig. 2b, c, respectively, also confirmed these phenomena. To further investigate the optical properties, a total luminescence spectrum (200–900 nm) was obtained, as shown in Fig. 2d. The excitation-emission matrix spectra suggest that the R-CDs possess excitation-independent emissions, removing the challenge of choosing an appropriate filter [42]. The absolute QY of the R-CDs was approximately 16.67 ± 0.85% (Fig. S3). In addition, the influence of saline solution and pH on fluorescence was also determined, and the results indicated that the R-CDs could be stable in NaCl solutions (0–200 mM) and solutions with different pH values ranging from 5 to 11. The detailed information is shown in Fig. S4. These results demonstrated that red-emissive.
R-CDs with a high PLQY were successfully fabricated, and excitation-independent emission could provide good support for anti-interference in cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TP by the R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The potential application of R-CDs for detecting TP, which contain four types of catechins (Fig. S5), was investigated. In this regard, the fluorescent behavior of the R-CDs with various TP contents was studied. TP can coordinate with Fe2+ to form new compounds with strong absorption at 540 nm. Based on the large overlap of the absorption and excitation spectra, the detection of TP is shown in Fig. 3a. With the addition of different concentrations of TP (0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 μg/mL) in 50 μg/mL R-CDs solution, the fluorescence intensity significantly decreased, indicating a good response to TP. The detection time was also determined and is shown in Fig. S6, where the fluorescence intensity upon the addition of TP was found to be stable within 1 min, indicating a rapid response to TP. Photoluminescence quenching was analyzed using the Stern–Volmer equation [43] as follows:
F
0
F
− 1 =
K
sv
c
=
K
q
τ
0
[ c ]
where F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the 50 μg/mL R-CDs in the absence and presence of TP, respectively. K
sv and K
q are the Stern–Volmer quenching constant and the quenching rate constant, respectively. While, [c] is the concentration of TP, and τ
0 is the fluorescence lifetime of 50 μg/mL R-CDs. During the detection process, the equation we obtained was F
0 /F – 1 = 0.028 [TP] + 0.045, which suggests that K
sv is 0.028 μg/mL (~0.061 μM−1). Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime of 50 μg/mL R-CDs was measured by time-correlated single-photon counting, in which τ
0 was determined to be 2.58 ns. The calculated K
q value was approximately 2.37 × 1013 M−1 s−1, which is much higher than the rate constant of dynamic quenching [30,44]. This indicates that fluorescence quenching is not a dynamic quenching process.

Moreover, the detection of TP showed a good linear relationship with R2 of 0.999 over the range of 1–20 μg/mL (Fig. 3b). The LOD was calculated using the following equation [45]:
LOD =
3 σ
S
where σ is the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity of the 50 μg/mL R-CDs without the addition of TP (n = 11), and the S is the slope of the fitting line. Based on the equation, the LOD was approximately 68 ± 0. 94 ng/mL (σ = 0.000638, S = 0.028).

Fig. S7a, b shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of different concentrations of TP with the addition of ferrous tartrate (pH = 7.5) and the aforementioned concentrations of TP with the addition of 50 μg/mL R-CDs. The results suggest that with the gradual addition of TP, the absorption peak at 540 nm increased. Furthermore, the addition of R-CDs could significantly enhance the change in UV–vis absorption; therefore, the detection of TP by fluorescence appears to be more sensitive compared with the standard curve method of ferrous tartrate. Moreover, compared to the reported sensors for detecting TP (Table. S1), the R-CDs showed a good linear range with high sensitivity which indicated a good potential application in real samples.

To verify the potential application of the R-CDs in the food industry, green tea, oolong drink, and jasmine tea drink purchased from the market were used as real samples to detect the TP content. The detection of TP in green tea using the ferrous tartrate standard curve method was a control. Briefly, the content and dry weight of TP in green tea detected by the ferrous tartrate standard curve were 1.69 ± 0.034 mg/mL and 135.20 ± 2.71 mg/g. In comparison, the content of TP detected by the R-CDs was 1.76 ± 0.035 mg/mL (140.8 ± 2.83 mg/g), with an error of 4.14%. An error within 5% indicates that the R-CDs have the potential for applications in the food industry to detect the concentration of TP. Thus, two types of tea drinks, oolong drink, and jasmine tea drink were used for further testing. With the addition of exogenous TP, the feasibility of R-CDs for TP detection was further investigated and the results are shown in Table 1. The TP recoveries in the green tea, oolong drink, and jasmine tea drink are in the ranges of 96.00 ± 1.26% to 106.00 ± 1.14%, 97.83 ± 0.45% to 104.50 ± 0.56%, and 99.00 ± 0.43% to 102.00 ± 0.63%, respectively. This indicates a generally satisfactory recovery and RSD, demonstrating potential applications of the R-CDs for TP detection.

To analyze the quenching mechanism of the R-CDs by TP, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, lifetime, size distribution, and zeta potential of the samples were investigated. As shown in Fig. S8, the large overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of TP with the addition of ferrous tartrate and the excitation spectra of R-CDs indicated the presence of IFE in the quenching process. However, the UV–vis absorptions spectra of R-CDs, TP with ferrous tartrate, and R-CDs with the addition of TP and ferrous tartrate ( Fig. 4a) suggest that a new compound formed due to the absence peak at 294 nm. As a feature of the static quenching process [46], the lifetimes of the R-CDs in the absence and presence of TP were further investigated. From Fig. 4b, the lifetimes of 50 μg/mL R-CDs (black point) and 50 μg/mL R-CDs with 60 μg/mL of TP were calculated to be approximately 2.58 ns and 2.35 ns, respectively. Because of the small differences in lifetimes, we proposed that the quenching process is attributed to the static fluorescence quenching mechanism and IFE. The hydrodynamic size of the R-CDs with TP (Fig. 4c) indicates that the diameter of them is increased from 2.90 nm to 689.90 nm due to the addition of TP. The zeta potentials of the R-CDs, TP with ferrous tartrate, and the R-CDs with TP and ferrous tartrate are − 24.07 ± 1.10 mV, + 2.58 ± 0.56 mV, and − 5.27 ± 0.26 mV, respectively (Fig. 4d). The absorb-ability caused by the charge also confirms that TP, which have enormous phenolic hydroxyl groups, was bound to the surface of the R-CDs, and eventually lead to the aggregation of the R-CDs. Therefore, the quenching process of the R-CDs originated from the synergistic effect of static quenching and IFE.

To investigate the feasibility of detecting potentially interfering substances, some metal ions and anions, including Al3+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, and Br-, as well as some bioactive molecules, such as Glu, GSH, L-cysteine, and L-tyrosine were added to the 50 μg/mL R-CDs solution to study the effect of these interfering substances on the intensities. As shown in Fig. 5a, the fluorescence intensities of the R-CDs slightly changed when 130 μM of Al3+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Br-, Glu, GSH, L-cysteine, or L-tyrosine was added. In comparison, 60 μg/mL (~130 μM) of TP caused a dramatically decreased in fluorescence. From Fig. 5b, the values of F
0 /F calculated based on the aforementioned experiment also show that only the addition of TP caused a significant change in the fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs solution. The results shown in Fig. 5c indicated that the R-CDs only responded to TP. Because before the addition of TP, interference substances were added one by one, or all the interference substances were added barely affecting the intensity of the R-CDs. When all the interference substances were added, the value of F
0 /F was 105.36 ± 1.21%, and the value changed to 266.01 ± 1.64% when 60 μg/mL TP was added. Therefore, the R-CDs have good selective performance for TP with potential applications for the detection of TP in complex environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and lysosomal targeting study of the R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of the R-CDs in cell imaging, a cytotoxicity assay was performed. In this study, WST-1 was employed to investigate the cytotoxicity of the R-CDs. From Fig. S9a, the viability of HeLa cells was maintained above 90% with different concentrations of the R-CDs added (0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 μg/mL), indicating that the R-CDs have good biocompatibility for cell imaging. Thus, the potential application of the R-CDs for cell imaging was investigated using a Leica SP8 confocal microscope. DAPI dihydrochloride and Lyso-Tracker Green were used for nuclear and lysosomal targeting and the results are shown in Fig. 6. Based on Fig. 6a-d, HeLa cells incubated with DAPI dihydrochloride and Lyso-Tracker Green exhibited bright blue and green fluorescence in the nucleus and lysosomes. Surprisingly, imaging by R-CDs with a high PLQY of red fluorescence showed a large overlap with Lyso-Tracker Green in the merged image, which indicates that almost all the R-CDs settled in the lysosomes of HeLa cells. To further verify this conclusion, fluorescence colocalization analysis was performed and the results are shown in Fig. S9b, the PCC was calculated to be approximately 0.91, which is higher than that reported in previous studies [47,48]. Moreover, the colocalization analysis results (Fig. S9c, d) revealed that the curves of R-CDs and Lyso-Tracker Green are almost identical, which further confirmed that the R-CDs could stain lysosomes with excellent selectivity. Furthermore, exogenous TP was added to investigate its behavior at a cellular level. As shown in Fig. 6e-h, the intensity of red fluorescence substantially decreased with the addition of 60 μg/mL TP, indicating that the R-CDs could detect the TP content in HeLa cells. However, when the concentration of TP reached 120 μg/mL, the images in Fig. 6i-l show almost no fluorescence signal of the R-CDs. Moreover, the effects of different co-incubation times were examined and are shown in Fig. S10. The results indicated that the R-CDs could enter the HeLa cells within 1 h. When the incubation time reached 3 h, the curve (Fig. S10x) showed great overlap between R-CDs and Lyso-Tracker Green, which confirmed the good lysosomal targeting by the R-CDs.

We further investigated the potential lysosomal targeting mechanism of the R-CDs in HeLa cells. The HeLa cells were incubated at 37 °C, 4 °C, or with 4 inhibitors including CPZ, genistein, MβCD, and amiloride. The incubation at 4 °C, or with CPZ, genistein, MβCD, or amiloride made the average fluorescence intensities of R-CDs decrease by 83.66%, 89.20%, 85.59%, 73.13%, and 68.61%, respectively (Fig. S11). Therefore, endocytosis of the R-CDs is energy-dependent and associated with multiple pathways, including clathrin-mediated endocytosis, caveolae-mediated endocytosis, lipid raft-mediated endocytosis, and macropinocytosis. These results demonstrate that the R-CDs can detect TP at the cellular level and could be applied to target lysosomes as a substitute for commercial dyes.

In summary, we prepared red-emissive nitrogen and chlorine co-doped CDs via a simple one-step solvothermal method using 4-chlorobenzene-1,2-diamine. The absolute QY of the R-CDs was as high as 16.67 ± 0.85%, and further investigations indicate that the prepared R-CDs have good biocompatibility and photostability in NaCl solutions and solutions with different pH values. For detecting the concentrations of TP, the R-CDs show a good linear relationship with an R2 of 0.999 over the range of 1 − 20 μg/mL TP. The LOD was calculated to be approximately 68 ± 0.94 ng/mL. Combined with the results from the interference experiments, we conclude that the detection of TP by the R-CDs follows a selective and sensitive pathway. In real samples, the detection of TP by the prepared R-CDs only had an error within 5% when compared with the standard curve method of ferrous tartrate, indicating their potential applications in the food industry. Moreover, the PCC of colocalization analysis of the R-CDs is 0.91 in HeLa cells, demonstrating a good capacity for lysosome targeting. Therefore, the R-CDs could be used to determine the TP content in foods, and have the potential to be used as commercial dyes for lysosomal imaging.

Lei Cao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing − original draft. Tongtong Zhu: Conceptualization, Visualization, Data curation. Minghui Zan: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis. Yulu Liu: Software, Data curation. Xiaoman Xing: Investigation, Formal analysis. Qing Qian: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Qian Mei: Methodology, Data curation. Wen-Fei Dong: Methodology, Data curation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Li Li: Supervision, Software, Data curation, Writing − review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (Grand No. 2018YFC2001100), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grand No. 21803075, 91959112, 81902166, 62027825, and 82172077), the Youth Innovation Promotion Association CAS (2022326), the Management Committee of Chongqing Liangjiang New Area (E1050J73) and the High-level Innovation and Entrepreneurship Talent Introduction Plan of Jiangsu Province (JSSCBS20211442).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132424.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lei Cao is currently a doctoral student at CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology (SIBET), Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), PR China. His research interests focus on fluorescent chemosensors (CDs).

Tongtong Zhu is currently a master student at CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology (SIBET), Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), PR China. His research interests focus on fluorescent probe for cell imaging (CDs).

Minghui Zan received his Ph.D. degree from Wuhan University in 2020. He is presently a postdoctor in CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology (SIBET), Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), PR China. He is interested in photophysics of dye molecules, and fluorescent chemosensors.

Yulu Liu is currently a doctoral student at CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology (SIBET), Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), PR China. His research interests focus on fluorescent probes (Si-CDs).

Xiaoman Xing received her M.S. degree from the University of Pennsylvania in 2008. She is presently a professor at the Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology (SIBET), Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), PR China. She is interested in bio-optics and physiological measurement.

Qing Qian received the master’s degree in mechanical electronic engineering from Shandong University, in 2010. He is currently an Associate Research Fellow. His main research interests include optoelectronic measurement, control technology and Maldi-TOF mass spectroscopy.

Qian Mei received her Ph.D. in Mechanical Engineering from University of Florida in 2007. Now she is a Research Fellow in CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology (SIBET), Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), PR China.

Wen-Fei Dong received his Ph.D. in Physical Chemistry in 2004, from the Department of interfaces, Max-Planck-Institute of Colloids and Interfaces, Germany. Now he is a Research Fellow in CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology (SIBET), Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), PR China.

Li Li received his Bachelor’s degree in Chemistry in 2009, and his Ph.D. in Physical Chemistry in 2014, from Jilin University, PR China. He is interested in photophysics of dye molecules, fluorescence resonance energy, and sensors. Now, he is a Research Assistant in CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology (SIBET), Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), PR China.
